title: Pilosella stuposa
taxonomy: Pilosella stuposa is classified in the family Asteraceae, specifically within the subfamily Cichorioideae. It belongs to the tribe Cichorieae and is closely related to other dandelion-like plants. The genus Pilosella is part of the larger group Cichorieae, which includes several species of plants commonly known as hawkweeds. Pilosella stuposa is further distinguished by its unique characteristics, such as its leaf shape and flower color. The classification of Pilosella stuposa reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the Cichorieae tribe.
uses: Pilosella stuposa has been traditionally used in folk medicine, although its uses may vary depending on the region. Its leaves and flowers have been reported to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. In some cultures, it is used to treat wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. However, more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness and potential side effects. Further studies are necessary to fully understand its medicinal uses and potential applications.

intro: Pilosella stuposa, also known as Alpine Hawkweed, is a perennial plant species belonging to the dandelion family, Asteraceae, characterized by its bright yellow flowers and hairy leaves. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with potential health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antitumor activities, which may help alleviate symptoms of arthritis, wounds, and certain types of cancer. The bioactive constituents of Pilosella stuposa include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Alpine Hawkweed is often used in herbal medicine to prepare teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as in the form of capsules and ointments, to take advantage of its medicinal benefits.
